narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your fourth-decile ranking in Year 1 grades (below the typical fifth-decile average) and third-decile ranking in Year 3 (also under the usual fifth-decile mark), alongside your LSAT score of 31 (well below the average of about 37), undergraduate GPA of 3.1 (slightly under the 3.2 average), upper-middle family income, status as a female applicant, White racial background, and plan to attend full-time. Compared to most students in our dataset, your key academic metrics like deciles, LSAT, and GPA sit in the lower range, while your demographics align closely with the majority—women make up about 44%, Whites around 84%, and full-time students over 92%. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the strongest push toward predicting bar failure. Right behind it, your LSAT score played a major role in tipping the scales the same way. Your Year 1 decile came next in influence, followed by family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—all contributing toward the failure prediction in that order, though with gradually less impact. None of these stood out as protective factors pulling strongly toward success in your case. It's tough to hear, but this highlights how early academic performance and standardized test scores often signal bar exam readiness for the model. Looking ahead, while your past deciles, LSAT, GPA, income, gender, race, and enrollment type are fixed, you can focus on building stronger study habits and bar-specific preparation now—perhaps through targeted tutoring, practice exams, or courses proven to boost passage rates for students with similar profiles. Retaking the LSAT if eligible could help future applications, as even a modest improvement there might shift predictions significantly. Many students in your position have succeeded by prioritizing rigorous bar prep programs early. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort—keep pursuing your goals.
